title: In lymphoma, where PET falls short, ctDNA delivers
gist: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) detection using Foresight Diagnostics' PhasED-Seq technology dramatically outperforms PET scans for predicting relapse in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, with a 30-fold difference in relapse likelihood between ctDNA-positive and -negative patients and 87% clinical sensitivity. Natera's Signatera platform will incorporate this technology for clinical use in 2026, and the NCCN has already updated treatment guidelines to include ctDNA analysis.

themes.theme: ctDNA superiority over PET in lymphoma

insights.claim: PET scans misclassify lymphoma remission status at high rates: roughly 20% of DLBCL patients with a negative PET result still relapse within three years, while around 50% of those with a positive PET result remain cancer free.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The diagnostic failure rate of PET creates a large addressable market for superior MRD detection technology; Natera/Foresight are positioned to capture this.
insights.quote: roughly 20% of patients with a negative PET result will relapse within three years. By contrast, around 50% of those with a positive PET result — indicating tumours are likely to regrow — remain cancer free

insights.claim: PhasED-Seq ctDNA testing shows a 30-fold difference in relapse likelihood between ctDNA-positive and -negative groups at end of treatment, far exceeding PET scan discrimination.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A 30-fold odds ratio is clinically compelling; this data point is the key commercial differentiator supporting Natera's 2026 launch.
insights.quote: There's about a 30-fold difference in the likelihood of relapse between ctDNA-positive and -negative groups at the end of treatment

insights.claim: ctDNA analysis stratifies patients independently of PET: only 7% of PET-positive but ctDNA-negative patients relapsed, while 69% of PET-negative but ctDNA-positive patients relapsed — demonstrating ctDNA's orthogonal and superior diagnostic value.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: ctDNA can both rescue false-positive PET results (avoiding unnecessary treatment) and identify true positives masked by false-negative PET — a dual commercial value proposition.
insights.quote: Of patients who had a positive PET result but no detectable ctDNA, only 7% went on to relapse; those who were PET-negative but ctDNA-positive had a 69% relapse rate.

insights.claim: PhasED-Seq ctDNA-MRD test demonstrated 87% clinical sensitivity for detecting relapse and 92% clinical specificity for identifying durable remission in large B-cell lymphoma patients.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: These performance metrics support regulatory approval and payer coverage; specificity above 90% reduces unnecessary CAR-T use.
insights.quote: The test demonstrated 87% clinical sensitivity for detecting relapse and 92% clinical specificity for identifying durable remission.

insights.claim: PhasED-Seq achieves sub-parts-per-million ctDNA detection sensitivity by detecting phased variants — multiple co-occurring mutations within a single DNA molecule — which has an extremely low probability of arising by chance, reducing background error rates that plague traditional single-variant MRD methods.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The phased-variant approach is a defensible technical moat; understanding the mechanism helps evaluate whether competitor ctDNA assays can replicate the performance.
insights.quote: The likelihood that two or more mutations co-occur by chance in a small DNA fragment is very low, which enables this approach to significantly reduce the background error rate
insights.locator: The little things give cancer away section

insights.claim: Even in patients with large tumors, ctDNA may be less than 10% of all cell-free DNA in the blood, making ultrasensitive detection essential — early ctDNA tests failed because they detected individual variants susceptible to non-malignant biological noise.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: This explains why legacy ctDNA assays underperformed and why high-sensitivity phased-variant approaches hold structural advantage over simpler tests.
insights.quote: Even in patients with large tumours, ctDNA may be less than 10% of all cell-free DNA in the blood
insights.locator: The little things give cancer away section

insights.claim: Natera acquired Foresight Diagnostics and will incorporate PhasED-Seq technology into an upgraded Signatera ctDNA-MRD platform for lymphoma, targeting clinical availability in 2026.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Natera (NTRA) gains a potentially market-leading lymphoma MRD product; watch for reimbursement decisions and FDA clearance as key catalysts in 2026.
insights.quote: A new version of Natera's Signatera platform that incorporates Foresight's technology will be available for clinical use in 2026.

themes.theme: Clinical guideline adoption and treatment decision impact

insights.claim: The National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) has already updated its B-cell lymphoma treatment guidelines to include ctDNA analysis to confirm PET-positive scans, a first step toward broad clinical adoption.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: NCCN guideline inclusion is a major reimbursement milestone; this de-risks Natera's revenue trajectory for the lymphoma MRD test.

insights.claim: Post-treatment ctDNA surveillance at one year showed that a negative ctDNA-MRD result predicted 97% probability of remaining cancer-free at three years, while a positive result was associated with 36% probability of relapse — enabling clinically actionable risk stratification during surveillance.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Surveillance utility extends the commercial opportunity beyond end-of-treatment testing to repeated monitoring, increasing per-patient revenue.
insights.quote: a negative ctDNA-MRD test represented a 97% probability of remaining cancer free three years later, whereas a positive ctDNA-MRD result was associated with a 36% probability of relapse

insights.claim: Two ongoing clinical trials (NCT06693830, NCT06500273) are evaluating whether ctDNA-MRD positivity after PET-negative results should trigger proactive consolidated CAR-T therapy — which if positive would be the first evidence that ctDNA can guide treatment decisions in lymphoma.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Positive trial readouts would dramatically expand the actionable use case and market size for ctDNA testing; watch Alpha3 trial results.
insights.quote: If the CAR-T therapy improves outcomes, it will be the first indication that this type of test can direct therapy in a meaningful way.
